llm_call.raw_output: {"tool_name":"submit_resolution","parameters":{"root_cause":"Root cause: postgres-primary (PostgreSQL 13.4) exhausted available connections (max_connections reached) causing the payment-api (symptom) to receive and return HTTP 500 errors. DB error observed: 'FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections' and numerous long-running backend processes in pg_stat_activity from user 'payment_user'. Contributing factors: payment-api replicas are configured with an excessively large client connection pool (payment-api v2.3.1, PG driver node-postgres 8.x) — pool size ~200 per replica across 6 replicas -> ~1200 concurrent DB connections, exceeding postgres-primary max_connections=500. No recent deploys recorded, so this is an operational/configuration scaling/connection-exhaustion issue rather than a code regression.","affected_service":"postgres-primary","recommendation":"Immediate mitigation (minutes): 1) Reduce connection pressure: set payment-api DB pool size to 15 (env PAYMENT_DB_POOL=15) and perform a rolling restart of payment-api pods to pick up change. 2) On postgres-primary, terminate excess idle connections from payment_user: run SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE usename='payment_user' AND state IN ('idle','active') AND pid <> pg_backend_pid() AND query NOT ILIKE '%pg_terminate_backend%'; 3) Temporarily increase max_connections to 1000 and tune shared_buffers if needed to avoid immediate rejections (apply with caution during low traffic window). 4) Short-term add PgBouncer (transaction mode) in front of postgres-primary and point payment-api to PgBouncer to limit backend connections. Longer-term actions (hours-days): a) Enforce a safe default DB pool size in payment-api deployments (e.g., 15) and add health checks that fail-fast on DB connection errors. b) Implement centralized connection-proxy (PgBouncer) and per-app connection quotas. c) Add alerting for DB connection utilization and pg_stat_activity long-running queries, and perform a postmortem to determine why pool settings were changed or why traffic spike caused saturation. d) Consider scaling postgres (vertical resources or read-replicas with write-scaling strategy) if sustained high QPS expected."}}

parameters.root_cause: Root cause: postgres-primary (PostgreSQL 13.4) exhausted available connections (max_connections reached) causing the payment-api (symptom) to receive and return HTTP 500 errors. DB error observed: 'FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections' and numerous long-running backend processes in pg_stat_activity from user 'payment_user'. Contributing factors: payment-api replicas are configured with an excessively large client connection pool (payment-api v2.3.1, PG driver node-postgres 8.x) — pool size ~200 per replica across 6 replicas -> ~1200 concurrent DB connections, exceeding postgres-primary max_connections=500. No recent deploys recorded, so this is an operational/configuration scaling/connection-exhaustion issue rather than a code regression.
parameters.recommendation: Immediate mitigation (minutes): 1) Reduce connection pressure: set payment-api DB pool size to 15 (env PAYMENT_DB_POOL=15) and perform a rolling restart of payment-api pods to pick up change. 2) On postgres-primary, terminate excess idle connections from payment_user: run SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE usename='payment_user' AND state IN ('idle','active') AND pid <> pg_backend_pid() AND query NOT ILIKE '%pg_terminate_backend%'; 3) Temporarily increase max_connections to 1000 and tune shared_buffers if needed to avoid immediate rejections (apply with caution during low traffic window). 4) Short-term add PgBouncer (transaction mode) in front of postgres-primary and point payment-api to PgBouncer to limit backend connections. Longer-term actions (hours-days): a) Enforce a safe default DB pool size in payment-api deployments (e.g., 15) and add health checks that fail-fast on DB connection errors. b) Implement centralized connection-proxy (PgBouncer) and per-app connection quotas. c) Add alerting for DB connection utilization and pg_stat_activity long-running queries, and perform a postmortem to determine why pool settings were changed or why traffic spike caused saturation. d) Consider scaling postgres (vertical resources or read-replicas with write-scaling strategy) if sustained high QPS expected.

parameters.root_cause: Timeline & root cause: At 2026-04-01T14:10Z the analytics-worker kicked off the scheduled job 'quarterly_event_analysis' which performed large full-table scans and long-running aggregations directly against postgres-primary (no read replica configured). This job ran concurrently with normal traffic and consumed many persistent DB connections, causing postgres-primary to exhaust available connections and spike I/O/CPU. Observable failure modes (seen in system traces and downstream symptoms): postgres-primary reached connection limits and returned errors consistent with "FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections" and long query I/O waits; auth-service began surfacing DB connection pool timeouts and "could not obtain connection from pool" errors and returned 5xx/503 to callers; user-profile-service and notification-service (notification-service v3.1.0 deployed 2026-04-01T14:02:00Z) degraded because they depend on auth-service and/or the DB. No recent deploy to analytics-worker was recorded (the job was scheduled automation), and notification-service deploy did not include DB schema changes, so the upstream analytics workload is the root cause.
parameters.recommendation: Immediate mitigation (do now): 1) Abort/kill the running quarterly_event_analysis job on analytics-worker to free DB connections immediately. 2) If abort requires DB-side action, identify and terminate the long-running SQL sessions on postgres-primary (pg_stat_activity) started at ~14:10Z. 3) Temporarily increase postgres-primary max_connections by ~50% (example: 200 -> 300) and ensure connection settings are applied during peak while watching memory. 4) Scale auth-service connection pool settings to cap waits (set pool_max <= available DB connections minus app consumers) and add short connection acquisition timeouts to fail fast. Medium-term fixes: 5) Offload analytics queries to a dedicated read-replica and schedule heavy analytics jobs during low-traffic windows. 6) Deploy PgBouncer between analytics-worker and postgres-primary, and enforce connection limits for analytics jobs. 7) Add statement_timeout/lock/time-based safeguards to analytics queries, chunk large reads (pagination) and add job-level concurrency limits. 8) Add monitoring/alerting for postgres connection usage, long-running queries, and job start events. These steps will restore availability fast (kill job + increase connections) and prevent recurrence by isolating analytics workload from production traffic.
